case_name: Microcystin control for drinking water production from impacted well water
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for well water containing microcystins intended for drinking water supply.

input_data.scenario_background: A municipal well field located near a eutrophic surface water body has shown episodic cyanotoxin intrusion into a shallow production well during high recharge periods. The utility needs a reliable treatment approach for finished drinking water and wants established processes that can operate stably without high energy demand. The well water is otherwise relatively clear but contains enough natural organic matter to affect adsorption and oxidation performance.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve effective microcystin control suitable for drinking water compliance and provide consistent finished water quality during episodic contamination events.
secondary_goals: use proven treatment technology for cyanotoxins
limit energy use and avoid unnecessarily complex treatment
account for DOC effects on toxin removal

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: spent media replacement and routine backwash waste handling are manageable, but complex hazardous residuals systems are not preferred

core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon_dosing: adsorb dissolved microcystins before downstream removal
core_unit_functions.rapid_mix_and_contact: provide controlled contact time and distribute PAC effectively
core_unit_functions.filtration: remove PAC particles and any associated solids
core_unit_functions.disinfection: provide final microbiological protection without being the primary toxin removal barrier

applicability_rationale: microcystins are the clear primary contaminant and PAC is a widely recognized treatment barrier for dissolved cyanotoxins
the well water has low turbidity, so PAC contact and downstream filtration are operationally straightforward
moderate DOC creates adsorption competition, but PAC dose adjustment is a practical and established response
this option fits a utility seeking stable, proven treatment without high-pressure membranes
potential_risks: higher DOC can increase PAC demand and shorten effective adsorption capacity
insufficient contact time or underdosing during contamination spikes can reduce toxin removal reliability
filter loading and backwash frequency may increase with PAC application

core_unit_functions.ozonation: oxidize dissolved microcystins to less problematic compounds when adequately dosed
core_unit_functions.biologically_active_filtration: polish oxidation byproducts and remove biodegradable organic matter

applicability_rationale: ozone is a well-documented oxidation barrier for dissolved microcystins
the contaminant is predominantly dissolved, making oxidation a technically strong fit
biologically active filtration adds resilience and polishing after oxidation
this is effective but somewhat more infrastructure-intensive than PAC-based treatment for a moderate-constraint utility
potential_risks: ozone dose control must account for DOC and other oxidant demand
formation of regulated oxidation byproducts such as bromate must be checked if bromide is present
operator skill and monitoring needs are higher than for adsorption-only systems

core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_adsorption: adsorb dissolved microcystins during fixed-bed contact
core_unit_functions.post_filtration: retain fines and polish finished water

applicability_rationale: GAC is an established barrier for dissolved organic micropollutants including microcystins
a fixed-bed system can provide stable operation appropriate for drinking water treatment
the well water matrix is relatively simple, though DOC will still compete for adsorption sites
this is suitable where the utility prefers a passive and robust adsorption step over chemical oxidation
potential_risks: breakthrough can occur if bed life is shortened by DOC competition
media replacement or regeneration planning is needed for sustained performance
performance during sudden concentration spikes depends on empty bed contact time and loading history

core_unit_functions.coagulation: improve solids capture and support removal of PAC and natural organic matter

applicability_rationale: this option adds conventional solids handling to a PAC barrier and can improve robustness where DOC and particle excursions vary
coagulation can reduce some matrix effects by lowering organic load before final polishing
it is feasible for drinking water treatment but adds chemical control and sludge production beyond what may be necessary for relatively clear well water
potential_risks: added coagulation may increase sludge handling requirements
poor coagulant optimization can reduce process stability and increase operating burden
for low-turbidity well water, the extra clarification step may be more complex than needed

core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_reverse_osmosis: provide strong physical rejection of dissolved cyanotoxins and other solutes
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust corrosivity and finished water chemistry after membrane treatment

applicability_rationale: membranes are technically capable of reducing dissolved microcystins
they may be considered where multiple dissolved contaminants also require removal
for this case, the main objective is a single primary contaminant with moderate constraints, so high-pressure membranes are not the preferred mainstream solution
potential_risks: concentrate management is a major burden
energy use and membrane O&M exceed what is needed for this relatively focused cyanotoxin problem
pretreatment and integrity management increase system complexity

preferred_rank_pattern: activated_carbon_based treatment should rank at or near the top for dissolved microcystins under moderate constraints
oxidation with ozone may rank highly but should not automatically displace simpler PAC-based treatment when stable moderate-complexity operation is preferred
high-pressure membrane options should rank lower for this case

must_reflect: preference for established drinking water processes rather than unnecessarily advanced high-energy systems

must_contain: the suitability of activated carbon for dissolved microcystin removal
the effect of DOC or natural organic matter on adsorption or oxidant demand
why membrane options are ranked lower under the stated constraints

should_mention: oxidation byproduct concerns for ozone-based options
need to avoid relying on routine chlorination alone as the primary microcystin barrier
